nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs; protected coastal waters at depths of 1–16 m. Exclusively associated with the bubble-tip anemone Entacmaea quadricolor.

diet.notes: The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish is an omnivore that accepts a wide variety of prepared foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, frozen carnivore blends, herbivore preparations, and marine flake food. Feeding two to three times daily is generally recommended. In the wild, the species feeds on zooplankton and benthic algae growing on and around its host anemone.

summary: The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish (Premnas biaculeatus) is widely considered the most aggressive of all clownfish species and among the largest, with females reaching up to 17 cm. Best kept singly or as a mated pair alongside a bubble-tip anemone in a reef aquarium.
careNotes:
The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish is the largest clownfish species. Females can exceed 15 cm, while males remain considerably smaller at around 6–7 cm. The gold stripe coloration is a genetic variant of the species: juveniles are born with white stripes that gradually turn gold over 9–12 months.

The species is generally considered easy to care for once established, though two points deserve particular attention. First, this fish is noticeably aggressive toward other clownfish, including others of its own kind, so only one specimen — or an established mated pair — should be kept per tank. Second, it has been reported to be somewhat more susceptible to common saltwater diseases such as ich and brooklynella than other clownfish species, making proper quarantine and stable water parameters especially important.

In the wild, this species uses Entacmaea quadricolor (the bubble-tip anemone) as its exclusive host. Captive specimens will generally accept other anemone species, but the bubble-tip anemone remains strongly preferred. The gold stripe variety is reportedly slightly less aggressive than the white-stripe form of the same species.

Like all clownfish, this species is a sequential hermaphrodite: the largest individual in a social hierarchy develops as female, and a smaller male can change sex to female if the female is lost. Documented pairs have been known to spawn for over 35 years, reflecting the species' exceptional potential longevity.

The trade name "Gold Nugget Maroon Clownfish" refers to a captive-bred color morph of Premnas biaculeatus with particularly prominent gold stripes that develop fully over 6–12 months. The "Peacekeeper Maroon Clownfish" is another captive-bred designer morph, selectively bred for reduced aggression alongside the characteristic maroon and gold striping of the species.

A separate line-bred captive morph featuring lightning-pattern stripes is covered under its own entry at [lightning-maroon-clownfish]; husbandry for that form is biologically identical, though trade availability and price differ.
breedingNotes: The Gold Stripe Maroon Clownfish is relatively straightforward to breed in captivity once a compatible bonded pair is established. The pair selects and cleans a flat surface near the host anemone, where the female deposits adhesive, demersal eggs (eggs that sink and stick rather than drifting in the water column). The male guards and aerates the clutch until the eggs hatch, typically within 6–8 days. Newly hatched fry are initially fed rotifers, then transitioned to Artemia nauplii (baby brine shrimp) as they grow. The most reliable way to establish a breeding pair is to acquire a proven mated pair, or to introduce a juvenile male alongside a young female and allow them to bond naturally.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Taxonomic and range data. Wild temperature range 26.7–29.3°C, max size 17.0 cm. Used for taxonomy (Pomacentridae/Blenniiformes), native range, and image candidates. Species listed under synonym Amphiprion biaculeatus on FishBase; Premnas biaculeatus is the accepted trade/aquarium name.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Maroon clownfish
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy cross-check and Wikimedia Commons image candidate identification.

fish.breedingNotes: Eggs are demersal and laid near the host anemone; the male guards and aerates the clutch until hatching. Fry require rotifers initially, then Artemia nauplii. Establishing a compatible bonded pair is essential before attempting to breed.
